summary: Martin Fowler traces the origin of the term 'antipattern' to Andrew Koenig's 1995 JOOP article, clarifying its precise meaning. The key distinction is that an antipattern isn't merely a bad idea, but something that appears to be a good solution initially before leading to trouble. This deceptive quality makes antipatterns particularly dangerous—sensible people adopt them because they look reasonable. Fowler also notes that context matters: the same solution can be a pattern in one situation and an antipattern in another.
key_points: An antipattern is specifically something that seems like a good idea initially but leads to trouble, not just any bad practice
The deceptive appearance of being a good solution is what makes antipatterns dangerous
Good antipattern descriptions should explain how to recover if you've taken the bad path
The same solution can be a pattern or antipattern depending on context
money_quotes: An antipattern is just like a pattern, except that instead of a solution it gives something that looks superficially like a solution but isn't one.
This is what makes a good antipattern something separate to just a bad thing to point and laugh at.
The fact that it looks like a good solution is its essential danger.
Since it looks good, sensible people will take the path - only once you've put a lot of effort into it will you know it's bad result.
It's useful to remember that the same solution can be a good pattern in some contexts and an antipattern in others.
key_insight: An antipattern's defining characteristic is not that it's bad, but that it's deceptively attractive—it looks like a good solution until you've invested heavily in it.
